title: edit: There is a lack of prediction power in most digital health acceptance models, and their focus is only on initial u…

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
edit: There is a lack of prediction power in most digital health acceptance models, and their focus is only on initial use, rather than continuous use which is essential to the management of chronic diseases. It is therefore crucial to develop a model that explains the continuous use of applications by the patient.
Expectation-confirmation theory (ECT) is widely used in consumer behavior literature to study consumer satisfaction, post-purchase behavior (e.g., repurchase, complaining), and service marketing in general (Bhattacherjee, 2001). The predictive ability of this theory has been demonstrated over a wide range of product repurchase and service continuance contexts, including automobile repurchase (Oliver 1993), camcorder repurchase (Spreng et al. 1996), institutional repurchase of photographic products (Dabholkar et al. 2000), restaurant service (Swan and Trawick 1981), and business professional services (Patterson et al. 1997). ECT holds that consumers’ intention to repurchase a product or continue service use is determined primarily by their (dis) confirmation of pre-use expectations that leads to satisfaction with the use of that product or service (Bhattacherjee, 2001). As a result, (dis) confirmation is a key player to decide whether to continue using the service or product or stop using it.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: The application of ECT to digital health acceptance models can provide a framework to explain why patients continue to use digital health applications. By measuring patients' pre-use expectations and confirmation of these expectations during the use of the application, healthcare providers can predict patients' willingness to continue utilizing the technology. This information can be used to improve the design and functionality of the digital health application to meet patients' expectations, ultimately leading to better patient outcomes. Therefore, future research in digital health should consider the application of ECT to develop accurate models for predicting patient continuous use of applications.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: edit: Based on the ECT, we argue that digital therapeutics user’s continuance decision is similar to a consumer’s repurchase decision because both decisions (1) follow an initial (acceptance or purchase) decision, (2) are influenced by the initial use (of digital therapeutics or product) experience, and (3) can potentially lead to ex post reversal of the initial decision. Hence, rational users most likely go through a non-trivial decision process, similar to that in ECT, prior to making an informed decision choice. However, in order to adapt ECT to a different context (i.e., digital therapeutics continuance), several theoretical extensions are required. Such extensions provide unique opportunities for theory refinement. Potentially they can explain digital therapeutics continuance decisions better than ECT alone.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Applying ECT to digital therapeutics, we propose that patients' decisions to continue using digital therapeutics are similar to consumers' decisions to repurchase a product. Both decisions involve an initial acceptance or purchase decision, which is followed by an evaluation of the initial use experience. If the experience meets or exceeds expectations, users are more likely to continue using the technology. However, if the experience falls short of expectations, users may choose to discontinue use. 

It is important to note that patients' decisions to continue using digital therapeutics are not always straightforward and may involve a non-trivial decision-making process. As such, theoretical extensions to ECT are required to adapt the model to the unique context of digital therapeutics continuance. Such extensions offer opportunities for theory refinement and have the potential to provide a more accurate explanation of patients' decisions to continue using digital therapeutics than ECT alone.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
edit: There is a lack of prediction power in most digital health acceptance models, and their focus is only on initial use, rather than continuous use which is essential to the management of chronic diseases. It is therefore crucial to develop a model that explains the continuous use of applications by the patient.
Expectation-confirmation theory (ECT) is widely used in consumer behavior literature to study consumer satisfaction, post-purchase behavior (e.g., repurchase, complaining), and service marketing in general (Bhattacherjee, 2001). The predictive ability of this theory has been demonstrated over a wide range of product repurchase and service continuance contexts, including automobile repurchase (Oliver 1993), camcorder repurchase (Spreng et al. 1996), institutional repurchase of photographic products (Dabholkar et al. 2000), restaurant service (Swan and Trawick 1981), and business professional services (Patterson et al. 1997). ECT holds that consumers’ intention to repurchase a product or continue service use is determined primarily by their (dis) confirmation of pre-use expectations that leads to satisfaction with the use of that product or service (Bhattacherjee, 2001). As a result, (dis) confirmation is a key player to decide whether to continue using the service or product or stop using it.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Existing digital health acceptance models have limited predictive power and focus only on initial use, rather than continuous use, which is critical in managing chronic diseases. Therefore, developing a model that explains the patient's continuous use of digital health applications is essential. 

To address this challenge, we propose applying the Expectation-Confirmation Theory (ECT), commonly used in consumer behavior literature. This theory has demonstrated strong predictive ability for product repurchase and service continuance contexts, such as automobiles, camcorders, and restaurant services. 

ECT argues that the consumer's intention to repurchase a product or continue service use is primarily determined by their confirmation or disconfirmation of pre-use expectations, leading to satisfaction with the product or service. Thus, the confirmation or disconfirmation of expectations plays a key role in determining whether users will continue using a product or service. 

By adapting ECT to the context of digital health, we can better understand patients' decisions to continue using digital health applications. More specifically, by measuring pre-use expectations and assessing the confirmation or disconfirmation of those expectations during use, we can predict the patient's willingness to continue using the technology. This information can then be used to improve the design and functionality of the digital health applications, resulting in better patient outcomes.
